reactions.label: value_modeled
reactions.basis: Documented values: child wellbeing (freedom from excessive test-prep pressure at age 10), predictability (stable, transparent admission rules), school choice (access to quality selective tracks without SES gatekeeping), safety (fair process). Value-modeled because the interest 'parents of young children' implies concern for these dimensions even where the scenario does not name them explicitly.
reactions.interest: Parents of young children (ages 6–14) in both high-SES and low-SES households; families in rural and urban settlements; rural families with high-achieving children.
reactions.public_good_frame: A child's achievement potential and wellbeing should not be determined by parental wealth or settlement at age 10. Fair, transparent, coachable-resistant admission rules reduce arms-race pressure on 10-year-olds and keep opportunity genuinely open.
reactions.argument: S1 appeals to us on its child-wellbeing target: replacing a high-stakes written exam with a less-coachable assessment could ease the test-prep pressure we see 9- and 10-year-olds experiencing (documented in media; felt directly by parents). Reserved seats for low-SES and rural pupils mean our high-ability child in a village or low-income family has a real pathway to selective education without moving or paying for tutoring. However, the scenario's own evidence section flags a fatal contradiction: 'a workable SES indicator' does not exist in Hungary's administrative registers. Our child's family might be classified wrongly, or the proxy might be gameable (parents misreport income; informal economy skews SES measures). If the system goes live with a proxy that fails, we face either false inclusion (our family is misclassified as low-SES, creating resentment among peers and parents) or false exclusion (a high-ability rural child is locked out because the proxy is noisy). Moreover, the 'less-coachable assessment' assumption is unvalidated—if it is still coachable (just less visibly), the arms-race shifts to informal tutoring that only high-SES parents can afford, and we gain nothing. The outreach assumption is also uncertain: funded campaigns may not reach Roma families or remote settlements if they are poorly designed or underfunded. If seats remain unfilled, the promised equity gain evaporates. Lastly, we worry about non-state (church, foundation) schools: if they absorb the displaced selective demand from families who exit the reformed public system, the rule change only reshuffles inequality, not reduces it—our child in a village might still lose out to a church-school track we cannot access.
reactions.condition_to_change: We would move to strong support if: (1) a pilot over 2–3 years demonstrates a workable, gaming-resistant SES indicator with ≥85% correct classification rates; (2) an independent study shows the new assessment resists coaching to a degree that closes ≥50% of the current test-prep advantage; (3) outreach campaigns measurably raise application rates from low-SES and Roma settlements by ≥40% without requiring our family to sign up for targeted programs (we do not want to be flagged as 'disadvantaged'); (4) a binding agreement with non-state maintainers locks their selective intake at current levels during the reform, so caps are not offset. We would move to oppose if the SES proxy is rolled out before piloting shows it works, or if non-state schools expand selective places faster than public-sector caps shrink.

reactions.label: value_modeled
reactions.basis: Documented values: predictability (parents plan school trajectories years ahead; shrinking seats and a decade-long transition create uncertainty), school choice (access to selective tracks is a valued option, especially for rural families who see selective school as the main route to urban universities), safety (our high-achieving child should not be trapped in a lower-performing school if a selective alternative exists nearby). The parental interest in 'insurance' or 'mobility asset' is documented in the scenario's own political-risks section; we model it as a core value for middle-class and aspirational low-SES families.
reactions.interest: Parents of high-achieving children, especially in rural and urban working-class families who see selective tracks as a route to social mobility; middle-class families who value selective education as a hedge against general-school quality variability.
reactions.public_good_frame: Early separation of high achievers into selective tracks harms peer composition for the majority and may reinforce SES sorting. Keeping more high-achieving pupils in common schools until age 14 strengthens peer effects for all children and delays SES-based sorting. However, this public good must not be purchased by denying high-ability children access to appropriately challenging education.
reactions.argument: S2 directly threatens our family's most valuable option: a selective seat at age 10/12. The scenario admits that 'caps may be offset by non-state maintainers'—and the political-risks section confirms this is the central unknown. If our high-achieving child is locked out of a shrinking public-sector selective track but a church school or private foundation expands selective intake, we face a bitter choice: pay for non-state school, or accept placement in a general school despite our child's ability. The scenario describes this as 'defeated caps,' and it is plausible (non-state providers have every incentive to capture displaced demand). We are also alarmed by the 'grandfathering' mechanism: it creates a decade-long two-tier system where siblings in the same family may have access to different pathways. This is arbitrary and undermines predictability—parents cannot plan. Additionally, the scenario's own evidence notes that 'residual selective places become even more SES-skewed as they scarcen' without a simultaneous rule change (S1-type admission reform). So S2 without S1 means that shrinking seats become even more hotly contested and even less accessible to our family unless we are wealthy. The peer-composition benefit is also contested (the scenario says 'weak'); no evidence shows that removing high achievers improves peer effects for the majority, and the international evidence on tracking is mixed. We accept that later tracking may reduce SES gradients, but not at the cost of denying our able child a responsive education.
reactions.condition_to_change: We would move to support if: (1) a binding agreement with all non-state maintainers (documented in a registry and enforceable contract) caps their combined selective intake at or below current levels throughout the decade-long phase-down; (2) an independent study shows that non-state expansion does not offset the public caps (measurable via school-entry data published annually); (3) the residual public-sector selective seats are subject to an S1-type admission-rule reform that prioritizes high-ability low-SES pupils, so scarcity does not simply amplify SES skew; (4) our high-achieving child is guaranteed alternative pathways (e.g., gifted programs, ability-grouped tracks within general schools, or selective upper-secondary entry at age 14) if excluded from selective lower-secondary entry. We would move to oppose if non-state expansion is permitted, or if the transition traps high-ability children in poorly-resourced general schools without differentiated instruction.

reactions.label: value_modeled
reactions.basis: Documented values: predictability (comprehensive reform to age 14 removes a pathway we plan for and introduces maximum uncertainty about implementation), school choice (structural abolition of selective entry eliminates an option we highly value), child wellbeing (teacher shortage and mixed evidence on peer effects raise the risk of our child in a under-resourced, mixed-ability classroom without adequate teacher support), safety (flight to non-state schools relocates sorting and may expose our family to higher fees or religious conditions). The parental interest in 'mobility insurance' is documented in the scenario's own political-risks section; we model it as deeply rooted in how families plan long-term education security.
reactions.interest: Parents of high-achieving children across all SES bands; urban middle-class families with resources to exit to private/church schools; rural families whose nearest selective school is the only high-quality option available locally; parents who have been told by schools or private tutors that their child 'belongs in a selective track.'
reactions.public_good_frame: A comprehensive system to age 14 with high-quality teaching and early diagnostic support can reduce SES-based sorting and improve equity. However, this public good is not realizable in Hungary without first solving the teacher shortage and ensuring that sorting does not migrate wholesale to non-state schools.
reactions.argument: S3 asks us to accept the largest structural disruption and the deepest uncertainty of all four scenarios. The scenario's own evidence section concedes that the central assumption—'sorting does not simply migrate to school choice between general schools and to non-state maintainers'—is directly contradicted by the equity_impact field, which calls this 'the largest risk.' We read this as an admission that the reform is likely to fail at its own goal: if middle-class and church-school families exit to private schools or demand selective tracks in non-state providers, sorting is not removed, merely relocate. Our family (whether high-SES or high-achieving low-SES) may flee to a church school, and the public system becomes even more stratified, with our exit now compounding the problem. Second, the scenario assumes teacher supply can be 'stabilized enough to staff heterogeneous comprehensive classes.' This directly contradicts the inputs' 'strong teacher-shortage finding.' We have seen teachers leave Hungary; villages have unfilled positions; and the reform would require retraining tens of thousands of teachers in differentiated instruction at a moment when the profession is hemorrhaging. If our child is in a mixed-ability class with an under-prepared or burned-out teacher, we face larger disparities, not smaller. Third, the equity gain depends entirely on contested mechanisms: the peer-effect research the scenario cites (Pekkarinen et al. 2009 on Finland) cannot be isolated from bundled teacher reforms and much higher baseline spending—Hungary would need to match those investments, which it has not committed to. Fourth, political durability is low: the scenario itself notes that a reversal is likely ('per the Polish precedent'). This means our child could be caught mid-transition if a government undoes the reform. We would rather not have our school-choice option abolished on a bet that subsequent governments will hold the line. Fifth, implementing this at scale while shrinking teacher supply will force school closures and busing in rural areas, which we see as damaging to community and child wellbeing (longer commutes, school consolidation, reduced local accountability). Lastly, the scenario does not address how families like ours—who have deliberately chosen or planned for selective education—will respond. It assumes we will accept the closure of this pathway, but we have not consented to it, and the political-risks section confirms we will mobilize. That mobilization is likely to succeed precisely because selective education is deeply embedded in how Hungarian families plan and in what teachers recommend.
reactions.condition_to_change: We would move from oppose to conditional if: (1) a pilot (not a rollout) of the comprehensive model in 3–4 districts over 5 years shows that sorting does NOT migrate to non-state schools (measured by comparing public-system SES stratification before and after, and tracking non-state enrollment flows); (2) teacher supply is stabilized—measured by vacancy rates dropping to <5% in disadvantaged schools—before the reform begins, and bonded by a binding wage commitment; (3) a causal study isolates the effect of delayed tracking from teacher-quality improvements, showing that delayed tracking alone closes ≥30% of the current SES-outcome gradient; (4) an enforceable agreement with all non-state maintainers commits them to comprehensive (non-selective) lower-secondary intake until age 14; (5) our child, if high-achieving, is guaranteed a pathway to an ability-grouped or gifted program within the common school, or early entry to a selective upper-secondary school at age 14, so that 'comprehensive' does not mean 'no differentiation.' Without these five conditions, we see this as a high-risk bet purchased by sacrificing a valued pathway.

reactions.label: value_modeled
reactions.basis: Documented values: child wellbeing (downstream compensation cannot offset the trauma and reduced motivation of being sorted into a lower track at age 10, nor can it alter the peer effects of early separation), predictability (accepting S4 signals permanent acceptance of selective entry at age 10/12, which is the precise mechanism we want reformed), school choice (S4 does not expand choices for our low-SES high-achieving child; it only tries to make the lower track 'less bad'). The scenario itself admits S4 'compensates downstream rather than altering the sorting mechanism'—we read this as proof that it will not work.
reactions.interest: Parents of high-achieving low-SES children in rural and urban settings; parents who reject the normalization of early selection; families who fear that 'compensation' packages are a concession that locks in stratification for generations.
reactions.public_good_frame: Keeping sorting mechanisms intact while funding lower-tier schools serves as a band-aid. It legitimizes early selection ('we have to pick who goes to selective school, so let's just help the others'), entrenches rather than dismantles inequality, and teaches our children that their life path is determined at age 10 by a test. Equitable education requires removing or delaying the sorting mechanism itself, not papering over its effects.
reactions.argument: S4 is a surrender dressed as pragmatism. The scenario explicitly states it 'compensates downstream rather than altering the sorting mechanism.' This is honest, but it is also an admission that the reform will not work. When our high-achieving low-SES child is sorted into the general track at age 10, no subsequent targeted funding, reduced retention, or curriculum standard will restore the peer effects, the label, or the trajectory that a selective track would have conferred. The international evidence (OECD, Portugal's own experience) shows that downstream compensation narrows the gap by modest amounts (3–8 percentile points in most studies), not by offsetting early sorting. Our child still carries the label of being 'general-track' for life; teachers and peers know it; our child internalizes it. Second, S4 legitimizes and entrenches early selection: by framing selective entry as inevitable and focusing public investment on 'helping' the general track, the reform signals that sorting at age 10 is normal, fair, and here to stay. This is exactly the opposite of what we want: we want early selection to be questioned, delayed, or reformed. Third, the scenario's own political-risks section notes that 'targeted funding is the first line cut in fiscal consolidation.' We have seen this in Hungary: targeted lines for disadvantaged schools get cut when the budget tightens. So S4's promised durability is illusory. Fourth, if non-state schools expand selective intake (as they likely will, per S2's uncertainties), S4 creates a two-tier system where families who can pay get selective education, and our child does not—and the government has already spent its political capital on 'compensation,' so there is no will left to reform entry. Lastly, S4 does not answer the moral question: why should a 10-year-old be sorted at all? The scenario dodges this by saying 'less disruptive' than S2/S3, but less disruptive to whom? It is maximally disruptive to the child sorted out of the selective track, and the research shows that disruption echoes for life (lower earnings, lower education, lower wellbeing). Compensation cannot undo that.
reactions.condition_to_change: We would move from oppose to conditional only if S4 is bundled with a hard cap on non-state selective expansion (S2's binding agreement) AND with a guaranteed pathway to selective or ability-grouped upper-secondary education at age 14 for any child who demonstrates high ability in the general track (measured by validated assessments, not by SES or prior school type). We would also require that 'targeted funding' is ring-fenced in the budget with a legislative minimum (e.g., 5% of total education spending) that cannot be cut except by parliamentary supermajority. Even then, we would view S4 as a partial measure, not a solution. We would move to strong support only if the scenario is reframed as a 'compensation + transition' package bundled with a 5-year pathway to one of S1, S2, or S3. S4 alone, treated as permanent, is a betrayal of equity.
